label: qwen36_35b_a3b_gguf_q4ks_w7900_cold_start_diagnostic

hardware.note: Diagnostic probe of the W7900 cold-start hypothesis raised by the 2026-05-23 README sweep. Persistent Qwen35GGUFResidentSession with N consecutive prefills, sampling /sys/class/drm/card1/device/pp_dpm_sclk before and after each prefill.

shape: 512 prompt tokens, single prefill per iter

rocm_smi_baseline.note: rocm-smi reports only two static DPM sclk levels (500 / 1760 MHz) on this W7900 driver, but pp_dpm_sclk observed up to 2977 MHz under load - the SMU boosts past the static DPM ceiling. The 'low-power state' warning from rocm-smi is the sampler observing the S/standby state at the moment of the read, not a sustained idle. (ROCm/ROCm#5849 + PR #2510 just tones the warning text down.)

findings: Persistent-session probe at 512/128 confirms a per-session cold-start: iter1 at 1416.37 tok/s vs iter2-8 mean 1573.30 tok/s (-9.98% relative to steady-state; +11.09% from cold to warm).
Adding a single throwaway wake-up prefill before the timed iters closes the gap: iter1 at 1553.41 tok/s vs iter2-5 mean 1577.11 tok/s (-1.50%); first-vs-steady is within bench noise.

Observed sclk under load reaches 2920-2977 MHz, well past the rocm-smi --showclkfrq DPM level1 ceiling of 1760 MHz. The W7900 boosts via SMU PMFW even when the static DPM table only exposes 1760 MHz as the highest fixed level.

The session-per-run bench harness (scripts/qwen35_gguf_bench.py) already mitigates most of the cold-start via `--warmup-runs 1`: the per-run warmup_run prefill+decode appears to leave the GPU warm enough for the next session's prefill to begin at near-steady-state, even though that next session pays ~60 s of model-load work before its first kernel.

Cold-start is only material at 512/128 in this measurement set: longer shapes (4K/32K/128K) have prefill_seconds >= 2.4s and the warmup_run vs measured_run prefill samples are within 1% (see /tmp/q4ks-w7900-bench/q4ks-*.json), so cold-start is not the explanation for the W7900-vs-RX-7900-XTX 4K prefill gap (1737 vs 2722 tok/s; -36%).

Setting `power_dpm_force_performance_level=high` requires root on this host (sysfs is 0644 root:root, the `video` group has no write access, and `sudo` is interactive). A no-sudo workaround is a small in-process wake-up prefill at session start, demonstrated by scripts/qwen35_gguf_bench.py's existing `--warmup-runs 1` and explicitly by this diagnostic's `--wakeup-prefills 1` mode.

recommendations.change: Promote --wakeup-prefills 1 (or a 50-100 ms small-kernel wake pulse) inside qwen35_gguf_bench.py before the timed prefill of the first measured run. The first warmup_run already does this implicitly via a full prefill+decode; nothing additional is required for the current 1 warmup + N measured protocol unless --warmup-runs is reduced to 0.

`sudo bash -c 'echo high > /sys/class/drm/card1/device/power_dpm_force_performance_level'` (or `sudo rocm-smi --setperflevel high`) pins DPM to level1 for the duration of the bench. Restore with `auto` afterwards. RDNA3 / Navi 3x supports this fully per rocm.docs.amd.com/projects/amdsmi perf-determinism.html.

The 4K Q4_K_S prefill gap (W7900 1737 vs RX 7900 XTX 2722 tok/s) is steady-state and not addressed by warmup. Diagnose with same-commit cross-host runs that capture pp_dpm_sclk samples during prefill, plus a HIP/clang version comparison between hosts. Filed as a future task; out of scope here.
